title: Osteoporosis
oneLiner: Systemic skeletal disease causing fragility fractures; common in postmenopausal women and older adults; requires risk assessment and treatment to prevent morbidity and mortality.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Spinal cord compression** from metastatic disease/abscess mimicking osteoporotic vertebral fracture — **finding:** bilateral leg weakness, sensory level, new bladder/bowel dysfunction; needs urgent MRI.
- **Pathological fracture** due to underlying malignancy (e.g., myeloma, breast/prostate metastases) mimicking fragility fracture — **finding:** lytic lesion on X‑ray, history of cancer, abnormal bloods (raised ALP, M‑protein, anaemia). May cause instability or bleeding if weight‑bearing bone.
sections.Presentation:
- Acute back pain after minimal trauma (e.g., bending, lifting) — hallmark of vertebral fracture
- Hip/groin pain after low‑energy fall — inability to weight‑bear, shortened externally rotated leg
- Loss of height (≥2 cm documented) or progressive kyphosis
- Asymptomatic until fracture — incidental DXA finding
- Exam: focal spinal tenderness, reduced spinal range, kyphosis; hip tenderness + limited rotation
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Osteoporotic fragility fracture** — typical low‑energy mechanism, no systemic features, normal inflammatory markers
- **Pathological fracture (malignancy)** — history of cancer (or new unexplained weight loss/night sweats); lytic/sclerotic lesion on X‑ray; M‑protein/paraproteinaemia
- **Osteomalacia** — bone pain (not just fracture), proximal myopathy, low vitamin D/high ALP/low phosphate; often bilateral femoral neck stress fractures
- **Paget’s disease of bone** — bone pain, skull enlargement, bowing of tibia; isolated markedly raised ALP; X‑ray shows thickened trabeculae, skull osteoporosis circumscripta
- **Multiple myeloma** — anaemia, renal impairment, hypercalcaemia, lytic “punched‑out” lesions; serum electrophoresis + Bence‑Jones protein
- **Splitter:** On spinal MRI, acute osteoporotic fracture shows oedema limited to vertebral body without posterior element involvement; malignant fracture often has soft tissue mass and posterior element signal.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Measure height (accurate with stadiometer), assess for kyphosis; check sit‑to‑stand test/frailty assessment if elderly
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile (Ca, PO₄, ALP)
  - Vitamin D (25‑OH), PTH (intact)
  - Serum electrophoresis + Bence‑Jones protein (urine)
  - TSH (if thyrotoxicosis suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First‑line:** DXA scan (lumbar spine + total hip) — diagnostic if T‑score ≤ –2.5
  - **For acute fracture:** X‑ray of symptomatic area (spine/hip)
  - **If malignancy/infection suspected:** MRI spine (STIR sequences) — differentiate acute benign from malignant; rule out cord compression
- **Diagnostic criteria:** DXA T‑score ≤ –2.5 (osteoporosis); –1.0 to –2.5 (osteopenia). **FRAX** or **QFracture** tool for 10‑year fracture risk in untreated patients (NICE CG146)
- **Never‑miss test:** For any fragility fracture in an older adult — **check calcium and vitamin D levels before starting treatment** to avoid hypocalcaemia with bisphosphonates. If atypical presentation (young age, high trauma, systemic symptoms) — **serum electrophoresis + urine Bence‑Jones** to exclude myeloma.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - For acute vertebral fracture: analgesia (paracetamol ± NSAIDs if no contraindication), early mobilisation as tolerated
  - For hip fracture: orthogeriatric referral, IV fluids if dehydrated, VTE prophylaxis, consideration for surgery within 48h (NICE CG124)
  - If spinal cord compression suspected: stat dexamethasone 16mg IV + urgent MRI; refer to spinal surgery/neurosurgery
- **Ward (first 24‑72h):**
  - Correct any hypocalcaemia, hypovitaminosis D (loading dose of vitamin D: [check local policy] — typically 50,000 IU weekly for 8 weeks)
  - Start bisphosphonate after calcium/vitamin D replete
  - Falls risk assessment + multidisciplinary therapy (OT/PT)
  - Check renal function before initiating bisphosphonates (eGFR >35 mL/min)
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - Lifestyle: weight‑bearing exercise, smoking cessation, limit alcohol
  - Calcium and vitamin D supplementation (Calcichew 500/400 BD or Adcal D3 on prescription)
  - First‑line pharmacological: bisphosphonate (alendronic acid 70mg weekly)
  - Alternative: risedronate, zoledronate (annual IV), denosumab (6‑monthly SC) — after specialist assessment
  - Consider bone‑forming agents (teriparatide) for severe osteoporosis or bisphosphonate failure (under rheumatology)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline 1‑2 L/24h if hypovolaemic (monitor UO)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC daily) unless contraindicated (e.g., high falls risk, consider mechanical)
  - Diet: normal oral intake if tolerated; NBM if hip fracture pending surgery
  - Observations: minimum 4‑hourly (NEWS2) — more frequent if hip fracture or on opioids
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line agent:** Alendronic acid 70mg oral once weekly — take with full glass of water on waking, stay upright ≥30 min, no food/drink for 30 min
- **Calcium/vitamin D supplement:** Adcal D3 (1 tablet BD) or Calcichew D3 Forte (1 tablet BD) — aim 1000mg calcium + 800 IU vitamin D daily
- **Alternatives:**
  - Risedronate sodium 35mg weekly oral (same administration rules)
  - Zoledronic acid (Aclasta) 5mg IV once yearly — require pre‑dose calcium (10 mmol) IV or oral ×3 days; check eGFR
  - Denosumab (Prolia) 60mg SC 6‑monthly — monitor calcium; do not stop without bridging (rebound vertebral fractures)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Oral bisphosphonates: oesophageal stricture/achalasia, hypocalcaemia, inability to sit upright 30 min
  - Zoledronate: eGFR <35 mL/min, hypocalcaemia, pregnancy
  - Denosumab: hypocalcaemia, pregnancy; caution with immunosuppressants (higher infection risk)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Bisphosphonates: avoid if eGFR <35 (zoledronate) or <30 (alendronate) — switch to denosumab (no renal adjustment but monitor Ca)
  - Hepatic: no major dose changes
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Alendronic acid **must not** be taken at bedtime or with food — causes severe oesophageal ulceration/perforation. Give with plain water only, and patient must remain upright 30 min. Zoledronate **must** have calcium levels checked before infusion — fatal hypocalcaemia can occur.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Hip fracture — immobilisation, DVT/PE, acute kidney injury (dehydration), delirium; vertebral fracture — acute severe pain, ileus, respiratory compromise if multiple fractures
- **Chronic:** Progressive kyphosis with height loss, chronic back pain, reduced mobility, loss of independence, increased mortality (20–30% 1‑year after hip fracture)
sections.Prognosis: - Hip fracture: 20–30% mortality within 1 year; 40% never regain independent mobility. Vertebral fractures: cumulative disability, but treatment reduces further fracture risk by 30–70%. With appropriate therapy, fracture risk can be significantly reduced.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Calculate FRAX/QFracture score** for any patient with a fragility fracture or aged >50 with risk factors — use before starting DXA if borderline.
- **Always check calcium and vitamin D before starting any bone‑active therapy** — correct deficiency first to avoid hypocalcaemia.
- **For hip fracture patients**, initiate orthogeriatric co‑management from admission — that improves survival and mobility.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Starting bisphosphonates without checking renal function or calcium** — risk of acute kidney injury, hypocalcaemia (especially with IV zoledronate).
- **Assuming all vertebral fractures are osteoporotic without excluding malignancy** — missing myeloma or metastasis. If red flags (weight loss, night pain, raised inflammatory markers, neurological signs), do MRI before treating as benign.
- **Stopping denosumab without bridging** — leads to rapid rebound bone loss and multiple vertebral fractures. Ensure ongoing prescription or switch to bisphosphonate.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** New or worsening back pain, leg weakness/numbness, loss of bladder/bowel control (cauda equina signs), inability to weight‑bear after hip fracture, severe constipation or abdominal pain (ileus with vertebral fracture), signs of DVT/PE (unilateral leg swelling, sudden dyspnoea).
- **Follow-up:**
  - DXA scan in 2–5 years depending on treatment and baseline T‑score
  - Review bone profile (Ca, vit D, PTH) at 3 months after starting therapy
  - Falls prevention clinic or OT home assessment within 4 weeks
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- Call senior / crash team if: NEWS2 ≥5, sudden hypoxia with hip fracture (PE), acute confusion (delirium), suspected spinal cord compression (urgent MRI), anaphylaxis/acute allergic reaction to drug (e.g., zoledronate), severe hypocalcaemia (tetany, prolonged QTc).
- Specific thresholds: GCS <15 or new focal neurology, lactate >4 mmol/L, HR >130 or <40, hypotension despite fluids, acute kidney injury (creatinine >2× baseline), febrile neutropenia.
- For hip fracture: expedite surgical referral if >48h from admission due to increased morbidity.

rawMarkdown:
## Osteoporosis
**One-liner:** Systemic skeletal disease causing fragility fractures; common in postmenopausal women and older adults; requires risk assessment and treatment to prevent morbidity and mortality.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Spinal cord compression** from metastatic disease/abscess mimicking osteoporotic vertebral fracture — **finding:** bilateral leg weakness, sensory level, new bladder/bowel dysfunction; needs urgent MRI.
- **Pathological fracture** due to underlying malignancy (e.g., myeloma, breast/prostate metastases) mimicking fragility fracture — **finding:** lytic lesion on X‑ray, history of cancer, abnormal bloods (raised ALP, M‑protein, anaemia). May cause instability or bleeding if weight‑bearing bone.

### Presentation
- Acute back pain after minimal trauma (e.g., bending, lifting) — hallmark of vertebral fracture
- Hip/groin pain after low‑energy fall — inability to weight‑bear, shortened externally rotated leg
- Loss of height (≥2 cm documented) or progressive kyphosis
- Asymptomatic until fracture — incidental DXA finding
- Exam: focal spinal tenderness, reduced spinal range, kyphosis; hip tenderness + limited rotation

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Osteoporotic fragility fracture** — typical low‑energy mechanism, no systemic features, normal inflammatory markers
- **Pathological fracture (malignancy)** — history of cancer (or new unexplained weight loss/night sweats); lytic/sclerotic lesion on X‑ray; M‑protein/paraproteinaemia
- **Osteomalacia** — bone pain (not just fracture), proximal myopathy, low vitamin D/high ALP/low phosphate; often bilateral femoral neck stress fractures
- **Paget’s disease of bone** — bone pain, skull enlargement, bowing of tibia; isolated markedly raised ALP; X‑ray shows thickened trabeculae, skull osteoporosis circumscripta
- **Multiple myeloma** — anaemia, renal impairment, hypercalcaemia, lytic “punched‑out” lesions; serum electrophoresis + Bence‑Jones protein
- **Splitter:** On spinal MRI, acute osteoporotic fracture shows oedema limited to vertebral body without posterior element involvement; malignant fracture often has soft tissue mass and posterior element signal.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Measure height (accurate with stadiometer), assess for kyphosis; check sit‑to‑stand test/frailty assessment if elderly
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile (Ca, PO₄, ALP)
  - Vitamin D (25‑OH), PTH (intact)
  - Serum electrophoresis + Bence‑Jones protein (urine)
  - TSH (if thyrotoxicosis suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First‑line:** DXA scan (lumbar spine + total hip) — diagnostic if T‑score ≤ –2.5
  - **For acute fracture:** X‑ray of symptomatic area (spine/hip)
  - **If malignancy/infection suspected:** MRI spine (STIR sequences) — differentiate acute benign from malignant; rule out cord compression
- **Diagnostic criteria:** DXA T‑score ≤ –2.5 (osteoporosis); –1.0 to –2.5 (osteopenia). **FRAX** or **QFracture** tool for 10‑year fracture risk in untreated patients (NICE CG146)
- **Never‑miss test:** For any fragility fracture in an older adult — **check calcium and vitamin D levels before starting treatment** to avoid hypocalcaemia with bisphosphonates. If atypical presentation (young age, high trauma, systemic symptoms) — **serum electrophoresis + urine Bence‑Jones** to exclude myeloma.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - For acute vertebral fracture: analgesia (paracetamol ± NSAIDs if no contraindication), early mobilisation as tolerated
  - For hip fracture: orthogeriatric referral, IV fluids if dehydrated, VTE prophylaxis, consideration for surgery within 48h (NICE CG124)
  - If spinal cord compression suspected: stat dexamethasone 16mg IV + urgent MRI; refer to spinal surgery/neurosurgery
- **Ward (first 24‑72h):**
  - Correct any hypocalcaemia, hypovitaminosis D (loading dose of vitamin D: [check local policy] — typically 50,000 IU weekly for 8 weeks)
  - Start bisphosphonate after calcium/vitamin D replete
  - Falls risk assessment + multidisciplinary therapy (OT/PT)
  - Check renal function before initiating bisphosphonates (eGFR >35 mL/min)
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - Lifestyle: weight‑bearing exercise, smoking cessation, limit alcohol
  - Calcium and vitamin D supplementation (Calcichew 500/400 BD or Adcal D3 on prescription)
  - First‑line pharmacological: bisphosphonate (alendronic acid 70mg weekly)
  - Alternative: risedronate, zoledronate (annual IV), denosumab (6‑monthly SC) — after specialist assessment
  - Consider bone‑forming agents (teriparatide) for severe osteoporosis or bisphosphonate failure (under rheumatology)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline 1‑2 L/24h if hypovolaemic (monitor UO)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC daily) unless contraindicated (e.g., high falls risk, consider mechanical)
  - Diet: normal oral intake if tolerated; NBM if hip fracture pending surgery
  - Observations: minimum 4‑hourly (NEWS2) — more frequent if hip fracture or on opioids

### Drugs & Doses
- **First‑line agent:** Alendronic acid 70mg oral once weekly — take with full glass of water on waking, stay upright ≥30 min, no food/drink for 30 min
- **Calcium/vitamin D supplement:** Adcal D3 (1 tablet BD) or Calcichew D3 Forte (1 tablet BD) — aim 1000mg calcium + 800 IU vitamin D daily
- **Alternatives:**
  - Risedronate sodium 35mg weekly oral (same administration rules)
  - Zoledronic acid (Aclasta) 5mg IV once yearly — require pre‑dose calcium (10 mmol) IV or oral ×3 days; check eGFR
  - Denosumab (Prolia) 60mg SC 6‑monthly — monitor calcium; do not stop without bridging (rebound vertebral fractures)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Oral bisphosphonates: oesophageal stricture/achalasia, hypocalcaemia, inability to sit upright 30 min
  - Zoledronate: eGFR <35 mL/min, hypocalcaemia, pregnancy
  - Denosumab: hypocalcaemia, pregnancy; caution with immunosuppressants (higher infection risk)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Bisphosphonates: avoid if eGFR <35 (zoledronate) or <30 (alendronate) — switch to denosumab (no renal adjustment but monitor Ca)
  - Hepatic: no major dose changes
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Alendronic acid **must not** be taken at bedtime or with food — causes severe oesophageal ulceration/perforation. Give with plain water only, and patient must remain upright 30 min. Zoledronate **must** have calcium levels checked before infusion — fatal hypocalcaemia can occur.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Hip fracture — immobilisation, DVT/PE, acute kidney injury (dehydration), delirium; vertebral fracture — acute severe pain, ileus, respiratory compromise if multiple fractures
- **Chronic:** Progressive kyphosis with height loss, chronic back pain, reduced mobility, loss of independence, increased mortality (20–30% 1‑year after hip fracture)

### Prognosis
- Hip fracture: 20–30% mortality within 1 year; 40% never regain independent mobility. Vertebral fractures: cumulative disability, but treatment reduces further fracture risk by 30–70%. With appropriate therapy, fracture risk can be significantly reduced.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Calculate FRAX/QFracture score** for any patient with a fragility fracture or aged >50 with risk factors — use before starting DXA if borderline.
- **Always check calcium and vitamin D before starting any bone‑active therapy** — correct deficiency first to avoid hypocalcaemia.
- **For hip fracture patients**, initiate orthogeriatric co‑management from admission — that improves survival and mobility.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Starting bisphosphonates without checking renal function or calcium** — risk of acute kidney injury, hypocalcaemia (especially with IV zoledronate).
- **Assuming all vertebral fractures are osteoporotic without excluding malignancy** — missing myeloma or metastasis. If red flags (weight loss, night pain, raised inflammatory markers, neurological signs), do MRI before treating as benign.
- **Stopping denosumab without bridging** — leads to rapid rebound bone loss and multiple vertebral fractures. Ensure ongoing prescription or switch to bisphosphonate.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** New or worsening back pain, leg weakness/numbness, loss of bladder/bowel control (cauda equina signs), inability to weight‑bear after hip fracture, severe constipation or abdominal pain (ileus with vertebral fracture), signs of DVT/PE (unilateral leg swelling, sudden dyspnoea).
- **Follow-up:**
  - DXA scan in 2–5 years depending on treatment and baseline T‑score
  - Review bone profile (Ca, vit D, PTH) at 3 months after starting therapy
  - Falls prevention clinic or OT home assessment within 4 weeks

### Escalation Criteria
- Call senior / crash team if: NEWS2 ≥5, sudden hypoxia with hip fracture (PE), acute confusion (delirium), suspected spinal cord compression (urgent MRI), anaphylaxis/acute allergic reaction to drug (e.g., zoledronate), severe hypocalcaemia (tetany, prolonged QTc).
- Specific thresholds: GCS <15 or new focal neurology, lactate >4 mmol/L, HR >130 or <40, hypotension despite fluids, acute kidney injury (creatinine >2× baseline), febrile neutropenia.
- For hip fracture: expedite surgical referral if >48h from admission due to increased morbidity.
